label: Chanokh — Part 3 — Chapters 56–73 — Epistle
summary_plain: The Epistle of Chanokh is his final message to his children and to all the righteous. He calls the sons of Metushelakh together and exhorts them: Love uprightness and walk therein. Do not draw near to righteousness with a double heart. He pronounces woes against the sinners and the mighty — those who build their houses with sin, who acquire wealth by wrongdoing, who change the words of truth. He gives the Apocalypse of Weeks — a summary of all history in ten weeks: Chanokh is born the seventh in the first week. The flood comes in the second. Avraham in the third. The Torah at Sinai in the fourth. The temple in the fifth. The temple destroyed in the sixth. The seventh week sees the wicked increase. In the eighth week a sword is given to the righteous. The ninth sees the judgment. The tenth sees the eternal judgment. He promises the elect: you shall shine as the lights of heaven, and the portals of heaven shall be opened to you.
summary_scholarly: Chapters 56 through 73 contain the Epistle of Chanokh, including the Apocalypse of Weeks and the birth of Noakh. The ACR reads from 4QEn-g (4Q212) — the primary Aramaic manuscript for the Epistle at Qumran — and the Orit Ge'ez which preserves the complete text. The Epistle is structured as Chanokh's testamentary address to his descendants. The Apocalypse of Weeks (ch 58) divides all of history into ten 'weeks' — seven past and three future. Chanokh identifies himself as 'born the seventh in the first week' — placing himself in the seventh generation from Adam, consistent with Bereshit 5 and the Yovelim chronology. The woes against the sinners (ch 62-67) parallel prophetic forms found in Yesha'yahu. The promise to the elect — 'you shall shine as the lights of heaven' (69:2) — is tied to the opening of the heavenly portals and the enthronement: 'I will seat each on the throne of his honor.' The birth of Noakh narrative (ch 72) connects back to the Watchers material and closes the circle with the flood.

key_terms.definition: Chanokh's final address to his descendants and to all the righteous. He exhorts them to walk in uprightness, pronounces woes against the sinners, and reveals the Apocalypse of Weeks.
Chapter 58. All of history divided into ten 'weeks.' Chanokh is born the seventh in the first week. Each week marks a major event — flood, Avraham, Sinai, temple, exile, judgment, the eternal age.
Chanokh says: 'I was born the seventh in the first week' (58:2). He is the seventh generation from Adam — matching Bereshit 5 and the Yovelim genealogy exactly.
Chapters 62-67. Chanokh pronounces woes against those who build with sin, acquire gold and silver unjustly, trust in their wealth, and change and pervert the words of truth.
The promise to the righteous: 'You shall shine as the lights of heaven, you shall shine and you shall be seen, and the portals of heaven shall be opened to you' (69:2).
The promise of enthronement for the faithful: 'I will seat each on the throne of his honor' (69:12). The righteous receive not just survival but honor.
Chanokh's name for the righteous line: 'Concerning the children of righteousness and concerning the elect of the world, and concerning the plant of uprightness, I will speak these things' (58:1).
Chanokh warns against it: 'Draw not nigh to uprightness with a double heart, and associate not with those of a double heart, but walk in righteousness, my sons' (56:3).

Chapter 72. The birth of Noakh is told with unusual signs — his body is white as snow, his hair white as wool, his eyes beautiful. Lamekh fears he is from the Watchers. Chanokh confirms he is legitimate.
The Dead Sea Scrolls manuscript that preserves portions of the Epistle of Chanokh in Aramaic. Found at Qumran Cave 4.

fill_blank.prompt: Love uprightness and walk therein. And draw not nigh to uprightness with a ______ heart.
fill_blank.source_quote: Love uprightness and walk therein. And draw not nigh to uprightness with a double heart, and associate not with those of a double heart, but walk in righteousness, my sons.

fill_blank.prompt: I was born the ______ in the first week, while judgment and righteousness still endured.
fill_blank.source_quote: I was born the seventh in the first week, while judgment and righteousness still endured.

fill_blank.prompt: Now you shall shine as the ______ of heaven, you shall shine and you shall be seen.
fill_blank.source_quote: Be hopeful — for aforetime you were put to shame through ill and affliction, but now you shall shine as the lights of heaven, you shall shine and you shall be seen, and the portals of heaven shall be opened to you.

fill_blank.prompt: Concerning the children of righteousness and concerning the elect of the world, and concerning the ______ of uprightness, I will speak these things.
fill_blank.source_quote: And Chanokh said: Concerning the children of righteousness and concerning the elect of the world, and concerning the plant of uprightness, I will speak these things, yea, I Chanokh will declare them unto you, my sons.

fill_blank.prompt: I swear unto you, that in heaven the angels remember you for good before the glory of the Great ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: I swear unto you, that in heaven the angels remember you for good before the glory of the Great One, and your names are written before the glory of the Great One.

fill_blank.prompt: YHWH will do a new thing on the ______, and this I have already seen in a vision.
fill_blank.source_quote: Then I answered and said to him: YHWH will do a new thing on the earth, and this I have already seen in a vision.

fill_blank.prompt: And now, my son ______, call to me all your brothers and gather to me all the sons of your mother.
fill_blank.source_quote: And now, my son Metushelakh, call to me all your brothers and gather to me all the sons of your mother, and the word of my voice will speak to them.

fill_blank.prompt: Know that the angels will inquire into your doings in ______ — from the sun and from the moon and from the stars.
fill_blank.source_quote: Know that the angels will inquire into your doings in heaven — from the sun and from the moon and from the stars — in reference to your sins.

fill_blank.prompt: And in your cry, cry for ______, and it shall appear to you.
fill_blank.source_quote: And in your cry, cry for judgment, and it shall appear to you.

fill_blank.prompt: And after me there shall arise in the second week great wickedness, and ______ shall spring up.
fill_blank.source_quote: And after me there shall arise in the second week great wickedness, and deceit shall have sprung up.

multiple_choice.question: In which week and position was Chanokh born?
options: The first in the first week
The seventh in the first week
The third in the second week
The tenth in the tenth week
multiple_choice.source_quote: I was born the seventh in the first week, while judgment and righteousness still endured.

multiple_choice.question: What does Chanokh promise the righteous?
options: They shall shine as the lights of heaven and the portals shall open
multiple_choice.source_quote: Now you shall shine as the lights of heaven, you shall shine and you shall be seen, and the portals of heaven shall be opened to you.

multiple_choice.question: What does Chanokh warn his children not to approach with?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Draw not nigh to uprightness with a double heart, and associate not with those of a double heart.

multiple_choice.question: What does the Apocalypse of Weeks divide history into?
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Apocalypse of Weeks — The First Seven Weeks of History.

multiple_choice.question: What does Chanokh's birth-of-Noakh narrative connect back to?
options: The Watchers and the coming flood
multiple_choice.source_quote: YHWH will do a new thing on the earth... some of the angels of heaven transgressed the word of YHWH.

multiple_choice.question: Where are the names of the righteous written?
options: Before the glory of the Great One
In the book of the kings
On the walls of the Mishkan
multiple_choice.source_quote: I swear unto you, that in heaven the angels remember you for good before the glory of the Great One, and your names are written before the glory of the Great One.

multiple_choice.question: According to Chanokh, who keeps account of human deeds?
options: The angels, through the sun, moon, and stars
multiple_choice.source_quote: Know that the angels will inquire into your doings in heaven — from the sun and from the moon and from the stars — in reference to your sins.

multiple_choice.question: Which DSS manuscript preserves the Epistle of Chanokh?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QEng (4Q212) — Epistle of Chanokh — Aramaic Primary.

multiple_choice.question: What does Chanokh call the righteous line?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Concerning the children of righteousness and concerning the elect of the world, and concerning the plant of uprightness.

multiple_choice.question: What is promised to each of the faithful?
options: A seat on the throne of his honor
multiple_choice.source_quote: I will seat each on the throne of his honor.

faq.question: What is the Apocalypse of Weeks?
faq.answer: A summary of all history in ten 'weeks' (ch 58). Chanokh is born seventh in the first week. The flood comes in the second. Avraham is chosen in the third. The Torah is given in the fourth. The temple is built in the fifth. The sixth sees the temple destroyed and the people scattered. The seventh sees wickedness multiply. In the eighth, a sword is given to the righteous. The ninth sees the judgment of the world. The tenth sees the eternal judgment and the new heaven. It is a compressed timeline of the whole plan of YHWH.

faq.question: What are the woes Chanokh pronounces?
faq.answer: Woes against those who build their houses with sin (62:4), who acquire gold and silver through injustice, who say 'We have become rich' and think their wealth is secure, who change and pervert the words of truth, who write lying words to lead people astray. The woes are directed at the powerful and the wealthy who oppress the righteous. Chanokh says their deeds are recorded — the angels inquire from heaven through the sun, moon, and stars.

faq.question: What does 'shine as the lights of heaven' mean?
faq.answer: Chanokh tells the righteous: 'You were put to shame through ill and affliction, but now you shall shine as the lights of heaven, you shall shine and you shall be seen, and the portals of heaven shall be opened to you' (69:2). After their suffering, the righteous become luminous — visible, honored, with heaven itself open to them. Each will be seated 'on the throne of his honor' (69:12).

faq.question: What happens at the birth of Noakh?
faq.answer: Chapter 72 tells of Noakh's unusual birth. His body was white as snow and red as a rose. His hair was white as wool. His eyes were beautiful. When he opened his eyes, the house lit up. Lamekh was afraid and went to Metushelakh, fearing the child was from the Watchers. Metushelakh went to Chanokh. Chanokh confirmed: the child is legitimate, he is Lamekh's son, and he will survive the flood that is coming because of what the Watchers have done.

faq.question: Why is the Epistle significant?
faq.answer: It is Chanokh's final testament — his last words to his descendants and through them to all the righteous in every generation. It contains the Apocalypse of Weeks (the only place the full sweep of history is compressed into ten periods), the woes against the sinners (the clearest denunciation of unjust wealth and perverted words), and the promise to the righteous (you will shine, heaven will open, you will be enthroned). It is preserved at Qumran in 4QEn-g (4Q212) and complete in the Orit Ge'ez.

faq.question: What does 'double heart' mean?
faq.answer: Chanokh warns: 'Draw not nigh to uprightness with a double heart, and associate not with those of a double heart' (56:3). A double heart is divided loyalty — approaching righteousness outwardly while inwardly serving something else. Chanokh's standard is single-hearted commitment: walk in righteousness, love uprightness, do not pretend.

faq.question: What do the woes against the wealthy reveal about covenant justice?
faq.answer: The Epistle of Chanokh addresses those who acquired wealth through the suffering of others and states that their wealth, structures, and claimed inheritances will become the evidence against them at the time of judgment. It addresses the oppressed directly: every act of injustice is documented. The cries of the displaced are heard. The record is kept in a form that no human institution can alter or suppress. The covenant justice system does not operate on the timescale of human institutional patience — it operates on the timescale of covenant obligation.

faq.question: Why is the Apocalypse of Weeks sequence disrupted in the Ge'ez?
faq.answer: In the Qumran manuscript (4Q212), the ten weeks run as a continuous sequence. In the Ge'ez, weeks 1-7 appear in chapter 93 and weeks 8-10 appear later in chapter 91. This is a Greek manuscript error — the text passed through Greek before reaching Ge'ez. The original continuous sequence was disrupted at the Greek translation stage. The Qumran Aramaic preserves the original architecture. This is why the three-witness method (DSS + Orit + Samaritan) is essential — comparing independent witnesses reveals where transmission errors entered.
